paper_title: DRIFT: A Residual Flow Adapter for Decoding Continuous Outputs in Vision-Language Models

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Many modern vision-language models (VLMs) build on autoregressive decoding of discrete tokens. While text-based output interfaces enable scalable pretraining and strong zero-shot generalization across diverse tasks, they are poorly suited for problems that require precise continuous outputs, such as localizing temporal boundaries of events or generating robotic control actions. To address this challenge, we propose DRIFT, a general framework for adapting pretrained VLMs to continuous decoding tasks. DRIFT combines a base predictor, which provides a coarse estimate of the target output, with a generative refinement module based on flow matching that iteratively improves the prediction. This residual formulation transforms the generative modeling problem from learning a global output distribution to modeling a localized residual distribution around a strong prior, substantially simplifying optimization. We evaluate DRIFT on both perception and planning tasks, including visual grounding and robotic control. Across multiple tasks and architectures spanning MLLMs, VLAs, and WAMs, DRIFT consistently outperforms a strong set of regression- and generative-based solutions.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Vision-language models (VLMs) [29, 1] pretrained on large-scale visual and textual corpora have achieved remarkable success across perception and planning tasks, leading to the emergence of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) [2, 10], vision-language-action models (VLAs) [20, 6, 5], and world action models (WAMs) [65, 62]. Inspired by the success of language modeling, modern VLMs typically adopt generic autoregressive backbone (e.g., Transformers [52]) and decode discrete text tokens as their primary output interface. This design enables scalable pretraining via next token prediction, and supports flexible zero-shot transfer to downstream tasks whose outputs can be naturally expressed as text, including classification, captioning, and question answering. However, many important perception and planning tasks fundamentally require predicting continuous quantities. Examples include estimating event timestamps in temporal video grounding (TVG) [49, 21] and generating control signals for robotic manipulation in VLA tasks [28, 25]. The discrete token interface is poorly suited to such tasks: tokenization introduces quantization errors, discards ordinal structure, and ignores fine-grained variation within bins. At the same time, replacing the discrete interface entirely is undesirable, as it would sacrifice the scalability of language-style pretraining and the generality that enables zero-shot transfer. In this work, we ask the following question: How can pretrained VLMs with discrete decoding interfaces be adapted for precise continuous prediction while preserving and leveraging the knowledge acquired during pretraining? Existing approaches only partially address this challenge. In MLLMs, prior work often discretizes continuous targets into tokens [20, 2] or appends special tokens with lightweight regression heads [69]. Co-first Author. Preprint. Figure 1: (1): DRIFT learns velocity field that transports starting distribution centered at an initial coarse prediction toward the target distribution, simplifying optimization by modeling localized residuals. (2, 3): Visualization of predicted action trajectories (on Libero-Long) and temporal event boundaries (on Charades-STA) under different decoding strategies. Compared to MLP or flow matching, DRIFT produces more accurate and stable predictions with lower variance. In VLAs and WAMs, diffusionand flow-based action decoders have emerged as dominant approaches for generating continuous outputs [6, 5, 65]. Tokenization and direct regression are fundamentally deterministic and thus struggle to model uncertainty in continuous prediction, including multimodal output distributions [8] and high-variance modes [37, 70] (Figure 1-2,3). Diffusionand flow-based approaches alleviate this issue by iteratively refining predictions through generative modeling in continuous space. However, they typically require substantially more training data and optimization effort, can exhibit high prediction variance in low-data regimes (Figure 1-2,3), and must devote modeling capacity to global structure that can be readily captured by deterministic predictors. Our key insight is that deterministic prediction and iterative generative refinement can form natural cascade. Deterministic predictors leverage pretrained representations to produce efficient coarse estimates, while generative refinement effectively captures uncertainty and recovers fine-grained structure around these estimates. Leveraging this insight, we propose DRIFT (Decoding via Residual Flow Adapter), general framework for adapting pretrained VLMs to continuous decoding tasks. DRIFT introduces lightweight residual flow adapter that augments base predictor such as token decoder or multilayer perceptron (MLP) regressor, with flow-matching-based refinement module. Rather than generating predictions from scratch, DRIFT models the residual error distribution around an initial coarse prediction and progressively refines it using flow matching (Figure 1-1). This residual formulation transforms the generative modeling problem from learning global output distribution to modeling localized residual distribution around strong prior, substantially simplifying optimization and improving learning efficiency. Building on this formulation, we develop general adaptation framework and effective training strategy applicable across diverse perception and planning tasks, provide theoretical analysis of its optimization advantages, and demonstrate strong empirical performance across broad range of continuous decoding tasks. We evaluate DRIFT on both perception and planning tasks. For perception, we study spatial visual grounding and temporal video grounding using MLLMs. For planning, we evaluate robotic control tasks using recent VLA and WAM architectures. Across all settings, DRIFT consistently improves over both direct regression baselines and standalone flow-matching decoders, while achieving stateof-the-art performance on several TVG and VLA benchmarks. For example, on TVG benchmarks, DRIFT achieves gains of +2.6% on Charades-STA and +2.1% on ActivityNet Captions compared to the latest methods [44]. In VLA tasks, DRIFT built on Qwen3-VL-2B [2] achieves 97.9% average accuracy on Libero benchmarks and 61.5% accuracy on Simpler WidowX. Our main contributions are summarized as follows. We introduce DRIFT, general framework for adapting pretrained vision-language models with discrete decoding interfaces to precise continuous prediction tasks. Our key innovation lies in residual refinement adapter that combines efficient coarse prediction with flow-matching-based refinement, together with theoretical analysis of its optimization advantages and an effective training strategy. 2 Through extensive experiments across visual grounding and robotic manipulation benchmarks, we demonstrate strong empirical results across multiple VLM architectures.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Vision Language Models (VLMs). VLMs learn from paired visual and textual data to address broad range of vision-language tasks. In particular, multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) [29, 2, 10, 22, 1] have emerged as powerful frameworks for learning transferable crossmodal representations from massive-scale image-text and video-text datasets, enabling sophisticated multimodal understanding and reasoning [11, 66, 23]. Beyond passive perception, another line of VLM research focuses on action control, leading to vision-language-action (VLA) models [20, 6, 5]. These models are typically pretrained on large-scale action trajectories to improve cross-embodiment generalization and can be adapted to diverse downstream manipulation tasks with limited fine-tuning. More recently, researchers have begun to connect scene generation with action control, giving rise to world-action models (WAMs) [65, 62], which aim to model both environmental dynamics and action-conditioned interactions. Many of these models follow the design of large language models, using Transformer backbones to decode discrete tokens. Our framework, DRIFT, builds upon pretrained VLMs with discrete output interfaces and equips them with precise continuous decoding capabilities. Vision Tasks with Continuous Output. Many perception and planning tasks require outputs that are inherently continuous rather than purely discrete. In this work, we focus on two representative tasks: (1) visual grounding [59], which localizes the spatial or temporal extent of visual concept in an image or video given language query, and (2) VLA tasks [28, 25], which predicts continuous robot action trajectories conditioned on visual observations and language instructions. Prior work has explored several ways to adapt VLMs to such continuous-output tasks. For visual grounding, some methods [2, 9, 31, 51, 2] discretize box coordinates or timestamps into tokens, while others [44, 38, 26] attach an MLP head to directly regress continuous values. For VLA tasks, existing approaches either use simple MLP decoder to predict action trajectories from VLM representations [69], or adopt diffusionor flow-matching-based heads [6, 7, 71] to improve action precision. Unlike these task-specific decoding strategies, DRIFT provides an efficient and general adapter for extending pretrained VLMs to continuous-output tasks. By refining continuous predictions around learned prior, our framework supports diverse perception and planning problems while preserving the reusable Transformer backbone of existing VLMs. Diffusion and Flow Matching. Diffusion models [14, 50] seek to reverse forward diffusion process that progressively injects noise into data by learning denoising function. Flow matching models [27, 30] simplify the training of continuous-time normalizing flows as supervised regression over target vector fields, leading an objective closely connected to diffusion models. While both frameworks were initially designed for generative modeling [48, 43], recent research has expanded their utility to continuous value decoding in VLA [8, 6, 61]. Our framework combines base predictor with flow-matching-based generative refinement module. We formulate this design as general adaptation framework for VLMs and provide theoretical justification for its optimization advantages.

Our goal is to decode continuous output Rc from VLM embedding without modifying existing VLM architecture. Our key intuition is that simple base predictor g, built on the learned VLM representation, can provide strong initial estimation and such estimation can be further refined by generative model. To this end, we propose learning velocity field vθ(y(t), t, z) that transports Gaussian distribution (ˆy g(z), σI) centered at the coarse prediction ˆy g(z), towards the target conditional distribution p(yz). Here σ Rc scales the variance of the Gaussian and is learned from data. In contrast to standard flow matching, which transports (0, I) to p(yz), our approach, illustrated in Figure 2-1, initializes starting distribution using the coarse prediction ˆy, offering strong prior over 4 Parameterization. We parameterize the velocity field as vθ(y(t), t, z) = yθ(y(t), t, z) y(t) 1 , (3) where yθ() aims to directly predict the target output y(t = 1). This parameterization has been recently discussed for generation tasks and shows competitive performance to velocity prediction [24]. Predicting the target allows us to add skip connection to the base predictor g. This is given by yθ(y(t), t, z) = α g(z) + (1 α) hθ(y(t), t, z). (4) hθ() is time-dependent and can be considered as the refinement module. It iteratively refines the residual between and the coarse prediction ˆy. α is learnable gating vector that selectively combines the outputs of the base predictor and the refinement module hθ(). Learning Target. We consider the following linear interpolant, also referred to as the bridge state, y(t) = (1 t)(σϵ + g(z)) + ty, (5) where ϵ (0, I). The target velocity is given by = dy(t)/dt = σϵ ˆy. We follow the standard flow matching loss in Eq. 2. We sample U(0, 1), construct y(t) by mixing noise ϵ with target output y, compute its velocity at with the corresponding condition following Eq. 3, and then minimizes the mean squared error (MSE) between the predicted velocity and the target flow v. Our learnable parameters include: (1) θ in hθ(), which models the residual error; (2) the gating vector α, which balances the coarse prediction and its refinement; and (3) the scaling vector σ, which controls the variance of the initial Gaussian distribution. It is worth noting that an alterative linear interpolant is y(t) = (1 t)σϵ + t(y g(z)). When the based predictor g() is fixed, these two interpolants are mathematically equivalent. However, the equivalence no longer holds when g() is jointly learned. In that case, they present two conceptually different ways of modeling. Eq. 5 first shifts the Gaussian distribution to σϵ + g(z) and then model the transport from the shifted distribution to the target distribution. In contrast, the alternative interpolant here direct models the gaps between the initial Gaussian and the distribution of the residual error. Practically, Eq. 5 offers greater flexibility in modeling. For example, it allows the skip connection in our refinement module (Eq. 4). Empirically, we validate that Eq. 5 provides minor performance benefits in our ablation studies (see Section 4.4). Joint Training with the Base Predictor. When the base predictor is already provided as part of the VLM, we either keep its parameters frozen or update them, based on the setting. When such predictor is unavailable, we instantiate as an MLP regressor, supervise it with an additional MSE loss Lp (with the overall loss as LFM + Lp), and jointly optimize its parameters. key challenge in this joint optimization setting is that the model can collapse to relying primarily on the flow matching model while ignoring the coarse predictor g. We find that stable learning requires applying gradient stopping to g() when computing the linear interpolant using Eq. 5. Inference. At inference time, we sample Gaussian noise ϵ (0, I) and initialize the trajectory as y(0) = g(z) + σϵ. We then solve the ODE dy(t)/dt = vθ(y(t), t, z) to obtain the final prediction y(1). Since our model is inherently generative and ODE integration typically requires only small number of steps (e.g. 10-20), when needed, we can efficiently draw multiple samples and average their predictions, which reduces prediction bias and often leads to slightly more accurate estimates. 3.3 Toy Experiments and Theoretical Justification We present results of DRIFT and vanilla flow matching model on several toy examples in Figure 2-2. DRIFT provides more faithful characterization of the underlying data distribution by effectively disentangling its modes. In addition, we provide detailed theoretical analysis in Appendix A, assuming fixed base predictor g(z). In summary, residual refinement strictly reduces prediction error compared with using the base predictor g(z) alone, as long as the bridge state y(t) carries information about the residual = ˆy beyond what g(z) already captures. Intuitively, y(t) is noisy interpolation between the initial prediction ˆy and the true target y, so it inherently contains partial target signal that the refinement module h(y(t), t, z) can exploit to better approximate r. sections.title: 4 Experiments and Results
sections.content: We evaluate DRIFT on VLA (Section 4.1) and TVG (Section 4.2) benchmarks. We further demonstrate the generality of DRIFT in Section 4.3, including its extension to 2D spatial grounding and its integration with world action model. Finally, we present ablation studies in Section 4.4. 4.1 Results of Vision-Language-Action Tasks VLA tasks aim to predict robot actions conditioned on visual inputs and language instructions. Given an observation of the environment and task description, VLA model generates continuous control signals for robotic manipulation. The predicted actions are executed by the robot, after which new observations are collected and fed back into the model, resulting in closed-loop control process. Experiment Design. We instantiate DRIFT individually with Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct [2] and OpenVLA [20] as VLM backbones. Qwen3-VL is recent MLLM pretrained on large-scale multimodal corpora, but it does not provide dedicated base predictor for VLA tasks. We use this model to study how large-scale pretraining benefits continuous action decoding and to evaluate the joint optimization of the base predictor and flow-matching-based refinement. OpenVLA, in contrast, is VLA model that predicts actions through tokenization. We include this model to demonstrate how DRIFT can refine predictions on top of an existing base predictor. Experiment Protocol. We evaluate DRIFT on Libero-Spatial, Libero-Object, Libero-Goal, LiberoLong [28], and Simpler [25] with WidowX robot arm. Both Libero and Simpler are simulation based benchmarks, with focus on tabletop object manipulation tasks. We train DRIFT on the LIBERO training set for LIBERO experiments, and on the Bridge and Fractal splits of OXE [41] when evaluating the policy on Simpler with WidowX. On Libero, we run 50 trials for each of the 10 subtasks in every suite. On Simpler WidowX, we run 24 trials for each of 4 subtasks. We report the action success rate as our evaluation metric. Implementation Details. We attach LoRA [15] adapters to the backbone VLM, including the vision encoder and the LLM, and jointly train DRIFT with the base predictor. Appendix B.2 provides full implementation details and hyperparameters. Baselines. We compare against two groups of baselines: (1) models with large-scale robotic pretraining, including OpenVLA [20], π0 [6], and π0.5 [5] and (2) methods based on adaptation such as VLA-Adaptor [57], VLM4VLA [69], and InternVLA [7]. Results. The results are presented in Table 1. With OpenVLA as the base model, DRIFT achieves consistent improvement across different sub-tasks on Libero and Simpler. Particularly, DRIFT raises the Libero average from 75.5% to 77.7% and Simpler from 7.7% to 13.5%. We report Recall@1 at IoU thresholds 0.3, 0.5, 0.7 together with mean IoU (mIoU). Baseline results are taken from ED-VTG [44], except for ET-Chat, whose results are reproduced using the released checkpoint under the same evaluation protocol. # Train denotes the size of the temporal grounding adaptation corpus. DRIFT outperforms ED-VTG, the strongest generalist baseline, on both datasets, with the largest gains observed on R@1@0.3 and mIoU metrics. DRIFT reaches 97.9% average success rate on Libero and 61.5% on Simpler, achieving new state-ofthe-art performance on both benchmarks. We outperform the VLM4VLA with Qwen3-VL-2B by clear margin, demonstrating the effectiveness of DRIFT. Yet, we see major gap between DRIFT initialized with OpenVLA and Qwen3-VL, which reveals that the pretraining of the VLM has strong effect on the downstream task. The general improvement in the vision understanding of Qwen3-VL provides strong action representation helpful for DRIFT, and thus yields better results. 4.2 Results of Temporal Video Grounding TVG aims to localize the temporal segment in video that corresponds to natural language query. Given video and text description, the model predicts continuous start and end timestamps indicating when the described event occurs. Experiment Design. We adopt ET-Chat [32] as the VLM backbone, following the ED-VTG [44] protocol for fair comparison with prior generalist baselines. ET-Chat natively localizes events by cosine-matching generated vid tokens hidden state to frame embeddings; we replace this matching head with an MLP that regresses (tstart, tend) from the same hidden state, providing the base prediction that DRIFT refines. The MLP, flow refiner, and LoRA adapters are jointly trained on TimeLens-100K [70]; Charades-STA and ActivityNet-Captions training splits are unseen, so all results are cross-dataset zero-shot. Experiment Protocol. We follow the protocol used by ED-VTG [44], whose standard splits enable direct comparison with the broader TVG literature. We train on TimeLens-100K [70]. We evaluate on the Charades-STA [12] and ActivityNet-Captions [21] test splits, and report Recall@1 at IoU thresholds τ {0.3, 0.5, 0.7} (R1@τ ) and mean IoU (mIoU). Implementation Details. Our implementation follows ET-Chats native video pipeline at 1 FPS, freezes the visual encoder and frame compressor, LoRA-tunes the LLM attention projections, and trains the residual-flow head jointly with the adapters. Further implementation details and hyperparameters can be found in Appendix B.3. Baselines. We report results for strong set of generalist zero-shot baselines on Charades-STA and ActivityNet Captions, while additionally including specialist models for reference. The most relevant comparison is the vanilla ET-Chat baseline evaluated under the same protocol, since DRIFT builds upon ET-Chat as its backbone. On ActivityNet-Captions, vanilla ET-Chat is weaker (R@0.3 24.1%, mIoU 18.9%); DRIFT lifts these to 57.9% and 37.3% respectively, surpassing ED-VTG on R@0.3, R@0.5, and mIoU. 4.3 Further Demontration 2D Spatial Grounding. We further adapt DRIFT to 2D spatial grounding tasks. Specifically, we attach DRIFT to the Qwen3-VL-2B model. We then train and evaluate the model on the RefCOCO series datasets [18, 36, 63]. As shown in Table 3, DRIFT consistently improves the performance of Qwen3-VL by clear margin across all benchmarks, demonstrating the versatility and generality of our framework. Further details are provided in the Appendix B.4. World Action Model. We further explore adapting DRIFT to recent world action models. Specifically, we integrate DRIFT with FastWAM [65]. Unlike the other models considered in this paper, FastWAM already employs flow matching based decoder for predicting continuous actions. In this setting, we introduce an additional base predictor using MLP and jointly optimize it together with FastWAMs original flow decoder. We train the model following FastWAMs training recipe, and present the results in Table 4. DRIFT consistently improves FastWAM on the Libero benchmark suite, further demonstrating the generality of our framework. Additional details are provided in the Appendix B.5. Qualitative Results. By progressively refining the coarse prediction from the base predictor, DRIFT generates more accurate actions and consequently improves task success rates. Similarly, Figure 3-right compares predictions from different decoders on TVG tasks. Exact temporal boundaries are often ambiguous. Through iterative refinement, DRIFT produces sharper temporal boundaries, leading to improved localization accuracy and overall performance. 4.4 Ablation Study We conduct an extensive ablation study of DRIFT on VLA on Libero-Long with Qwen3-VL-2B as the base model. Implementation details are the same as the training setup as in Section 4.1. Decoder Structure and Prediction Variance. We compare DRIFT against MLP regression, Flow Matching [27], and Diffusion [42] decoders. Table 5(a) summarizes the results, averaged across three runs. Both Flow Matching and Diffusion provide strong generative baselines for continuous decoding. Nevertheless, DRIFT consistently achieves clear improvements over both approaches. DRIFT Learns Improved Action Representations. Table 5(b) shows the effects of jointly training the base predictor with the flow matching based refinement module. standalone MLP baseline achieves 88.2% on Libero-Long, while the same MLP, when co-trained as the base predictor within DRIFT, improves to 93.0%. Incorporating the full DRIFT decoder further boosts performance to 96.4%. We hypothesize that joint training encourages the VLM to learn more informative action representations, thereby improving the performance of the base predictor itself. Model Design. Finally, we compare the main design choices inside DRIFT on Libero-Long with Qwen3-VL-2B, using the same setup as Section 4.1 except for shorter 30K-step training schedule. We vary (1) the linear interpolant: yt = (1 t)ϵ + t(y ˆy) (Direct Flow) vs. our design (DRIFT) yt = (1 t)(ϵ + ˆy) + ty in Eq. 5, (2) velocity prediction (v-pred) vs. signal prediction (x-pred), (3) skip connection vs. no skip connection, and (4) with or without the base predictor loss Lp. When without Lp loss, we allow the gradients from refinement module to back-propagate to the base predictor. Table 5(c) presents the design comparison. Among the valid configurations, the best result is our current design, x-prediction, skip-connection variant with Lp, which reaches 93.8%. Without the skip connection, the same interpolant with x-pred reaches 93.4%, still outperforming the Direct Flow alternatives at 91.2%. The effect of Lp is modest: with the skip connection, it changes 93.6% to 93.8%, while in the no-skip setting, the best score is obtained without it.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: In this paper, we presented DRIFT, general framework for adapting pretrained VLMs to continuous decoding tasks. DRIFT introduces lightweight residual flow adapter that augments base predictor with flow-matching-based refinement module. It progressively refines an initial coarse prediction by modeling residual errors around strong prior. This residual formulation simplifies generative modeling, improves optimization and learning efficiency, and enables effective continuous decoding across diverse tasks and architectures. Building on this, we develop general adaptation framework and training strategy, supported by theoretical analysis of its optimization advantages. Through extensive experiments on visual grounding and robotic control tasks spanning MLLMs, VLAs, 9 and WAMs, we demonstrate consistent improvements over strong regressionand generative-based baselines. We hope our work can shed light on post-training strategies for extending VLMs beyond discrete token generation toward more precise and expressive continuous outputs. Limitation and Future Work. DRIFT relies on the presence of sufficiently informative base predictor, and thus assumes that the underlying VLM already encodes representations relevant to the target continuous decoding task. In settings where such priors are weak or unavailable, the benefits of residual refinement may diminish. Further, our VLA experiments are limited to simulation environments. While such benchmarks are standard in VLA research, extending DRIFT to real-world robotic systems and studying sim-to-real transfer remain important directions for future work.

(13)), A.4 Comparison with Direct Flow Matching We next compare DRIFT with direct full-target flow matching. The purpose of this subsection is not to claim that residualization changes the unrestricted population optimum. In fact, the two objectives are Bayes-equivalent after deterministic shift of the bridge state. The advantage suggested by the teaser visualization is instead geometric and statistical: after the base predictor removes the predictable component of the target, the flow refiner learns smaller residual correction and its bridge remains localized around the coarse anchor. This distinction is important when the target has already been normalized to bounded range such as [1, 1]c; worst-case range bounds alone do not explain the advantage, but residual second moments and bridge radii still can. Theorem 7 formalizes the qualitative picture in which residual refinement stays in smaller tube around the coarse anchor, while direct generative decoding must transport noise toward the full target region. The statement is intentionally about the correction and the bridge geometry, not about artificially reducing the final conditional output diversity. At the Bayes optimum, the final conditional distribution should still match the true target distribution; DRIFT changes which part of the mapping is learned by the deterministic coarse prediction and which part is left to the generative refiner.

sections.title: B Implementation Details
sections.content: B.1 DRIFT Architecture Figure 4 illustrates the architecture of DRIFT. DRIFT consists of two main components: Base Predictor and Flow Refiner. The Base Predictor first produces coarse continuous estimate ˆy from the MLLM representation. The Flow Refiner then iteratively predicts the remaining residual from ˆy to the ground-truth target. The Flow Refiner contains three components: embedders, self-attention layers, and an output head. The embedders map the condition representation, the flow time t, and the bridge state yt into shared hidden space. The self-attention layers aggregate information across these embedded tokens. Finally, the output head maps the updated representation to the flow prediction, which is combined with the ˆy through the skip connection to produce the final prediction. 19 Figure 4: The overview of the DRIFTs structure. B.2 VLA Implementation Details Training Data. We follow the InternVLA-M1 [7] to prepare the training data. For the experiments on Libero [28], we train on the Spatial, Object, Goal, and Long suites. Spatial, Object, Goal, and Long contain 432, 454, 428, and 379 training trajectories, respectively, and yield 53K, 67K, 52K, and 101K training samples, respectively. We normalize the action trajectories with the max and min values. For the experiments on Simpler [25] WidowX, we follow the setting used in [7] and train the model on the Bridge and Fractal splits of Open X-Embodiment (OXE) [41]. Bridge and Fractal contain 60K and 87K training trajectories, respectively, and yield 1.28M and 3.4M training samples, respectively. We normalize the action trajectories with the 99th percentile value for the trajectories. Base VLMs. We use Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct [2] and OpenVLA [20] as the base models. For OpenVLA, when training and evaluating on Simpler, we use the checkpoint pretrained on OXE. When training and evaluating on each Libero suite, we use the corresponding Libero-finetuned OpenVLA checkpoint. During DRIFT adaptation, we add LoRA [15] adapters to both the vision encoder and the LLM backbone. We use LoRA rank 32, α= 64 for Libero experiments and rank 128, α= 256 for Simpler experiments. Special Tokens. We add one additional special token. For Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct, we use the special token <think>, which already exists in the tokenizer but is not used during pre-training. For OpenVLA, we add special token by extending the tokenizer, and we use both the added special token and the action tokens in the OpenVLA output as special tokens. We extract the hidden state of the special token from the last LLM layer as the condition zs. Base Predictor. When applying DRIFT to Qwen3-VL, we train learnable MLP as the Base Predictor, which directly predicts continuous action ˆy. We optimize this Base Predictor with MSE loss. When applying DRIFT to OpenVLA, we use OpenVLAs tokenizer as the Base Predictor. The tokenizer discretizes each continuous value into 256 bins represented by 256 action tokens. During training, we sample the action from the multinomial distribution predicted by the model and use it as ˆy. We optimize the tokenizer-based Base Predictor with negative log-likelihood loss. Flow Refiner. The Flow Refiner uses 4 self-attention layers with 8 heads and hidden dimension of 768. We optimize the Flow Refiner with MSE loss between the target velocity and the predicted velocity vθ(y(t), t, z). Training Hyper-parameters. We leverage both the main view and the wrist view to train DRIFT with Qwen3-VL-2B [2], and DRIFT learns to predict the next 8 and 4 consecutive actions in the future for the Libero and Simpler settings, respectively. We only use the main view when training DRIFT with OpenVLA [20], and DRIFT predicts one future action to match the original setting of OpenVLA. We train DRIFT with batch size 16 and learning rate 4 105 for 30K steps on Spatial, Object, and Goal, and for 60K steps on Long. For Simpler WidowX, we train with batch size 128, learning rate 4 105, and 50K steps. We use cosine learning-rate decay with 10% warmup steps. All the experiments are trained with AdamW [33] optimizer on 8*H100 in 8-16 hours. 20 Inference and Evaluation. We use = 10 forward-Euler steps and one stochastic noise sample per query. We evaluate DRIFT on Libero-Spatial, Libero-Object, Libero-Goal, Libero-Long, and Simpler WidowX. On Libero, we run 50 trials for each of the 10 subtasks in every suite. On Simpler WidowX, we run 24 trials for each of 4 subtasks. We report the success rate as the metric. B.3 TVG Implementation Details We provide the implementation details for DRIFT on Temporal Video Grounding (TVG). We evaluate DRIFT under the ED-VTG protocol with ET-Chat as the MLLM backbone. Training Data. We train the TVG variant of DRIFT on TimeLens-100K [70]. TimeLens-100K contains approximately 20K source videos and 100K querysegment temporal grounding annotations. Its source videos are collected from mixture of video-language datasets, including CosMo-Cap [54], InternVid-VTime [56], DiDeMo [13], QuerYD [40], and HiREST [67]. The temporal-grounding annotations are MLLM-generated end-to-end: an MLLM first identifies temporally distributed events in each video and then produces grounding queries with timestamp pairs. We use TimeLens-100K only as the training corpus and evaluate on the ED-VTG benchmarks, Charades-STA [12] and ActivityNet-Captions [21]. The TimeLens-100K training annotations are distinct from the evaluation annotations used in these benchmarks. Base VLMs. We use ET-Chat [32] as the backbone. We freeze the ET-Chat visual encoder and frame compressor, apply LoRA to the LLM attention projections, and train the LoRA adapters jointly with the Flow Refiner. We add three temporal special tokens, <CT>, <VALUE>, and </CT>, and train the LLM with its standard next-token objective to emit these markers. We use LoRA rank 32, α = 64, and dropout 0.05. Input and Condition Representation. We follow the ET-Chat video pipeline: videos are sampled at 1 FPS, encoded with EVA-CLIP ViT-G/14, compressed by ET-Chats frame compressor, and then fed into Phi-3-Mini-4K-Instruct (3.8B) as the LLM backbone. The frame compressor consists of Q-Former with learnable queries, context aggregator, and projector. For temporal grounding, the target text marks each continuous boundary with <CT><VALUE></CT>. We use the LLM hidden states at the <VALUE> positions as condition zs; Base Predictor. The coarse prediction is produced by small MLP head over the <VALUE> hidden states. The prior head predicts the temporal segment in center-width coordinates, applies 1.2 width expansion factor, and converts the result to start-end coordinates. Flow Refiner. The Flow Refiner uses the 2-layer self-attention described above with GELU activations, nhead = 16, hidden dimension equal to LLMs hidden_size/2. It represents each temporal segment in center-width coordinates and predicts the velocity in residual center-width coordinates. Training Hyper-parameters. We train DRIFT on TimeLens-100K with per-device batch size 1, learning rate 5 104 for 4 epochs with AdamW optimization on 8 A100 GPUs. The training run takes approximately 10 hours. The training objective uses flow MSE on the velocity. Inference and Evaluation. The start and end timestamps are normalized to [1, 1] by the video duration during training. At inference time, we unnormalize the prediction back to seconds and clip the final (ts, te) to the valid range [0, duration]. We use = 10 forward-Euler steps. Integration is performed in center-width coordinates; the result is converted back to start-end coordinates and then clipped to the valid video-time range. ET-Chat [32] is instruction-tuned on E.T. Instruct 164K. According to the ET-Chat paper, its TVG samples are drawn from DiDeMo, TACoS, QueryD, and NaQ. ET-Chat does not report ActivityNetCaptions performance in the original paper, so we obtain the ET-Chat baseline in Table 2 by running the released checkpoint under the ED-VTG evaluation protocol. We report Recall@1 at different IoU thresholds and mean IoU. Recall@1 at IoU threshold τ measures the fraction of queries whose top-1 predicted interval has IoU greater than τ with the ground-truth interval. mIoU is the mean IoU over all evaluated queries. 21 B.4 Spatial Grounding Implementation Details Training Data. We use the training set of the RefCOCO [18], RefCOCO+ [36], and RefCOCOg [63], which contains 120K, 120K, and 80K training samples, respectively. All the bounding boxes coordinates are normalized between 0 and 1, and with the bounding box format of [cx,cy,w,h]. Base VLM. We use Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct as the base model. We add the LoRA [15] adaptor with rank 32, α= 64, on both the vision encoder and the LLM backbone. Special Tokens. We add one additional special token <think>, which already exists in the tokenizer but was never used during training in the Instruct version of the model. We extract the hidden state of the special token from the last layer of the LLM as condition c. Base Predictor. We train learnable MLP as the base predictor, which directly predicts ˆy. We apply MSE loss to update the base predictor. Flow Refiner. We use 8-layer self-attention, with 8 heads and hidden dimension of 768 in the flow refiner. We optimize the Flow Refiner with MSE loss between the target velocity and the predicted velocity vθ(y(t), t, z). Training Hyper-parameters. We train DRIFT with batch size 64 and learning rate 4 105 for 1 epoch (about 5K steps). We use cosine-decay learning rate scheduling and with 3% warmup steps. All the experiments are trained with AdamW optimizer on 8*A100 in 2 hours. Inference and evaluation = 10 forward-Euler steps; one stochastic noise sample per query (deterministic). Following the setting used in the Qwen3-VL, we evaluate the validation set of DRIFT on RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, and RefCOCOg, and report the Accuracy@1 as the metric. B.5 FastWAM VLA Implementation Details Training Data. Following the training setting of the FastWAM [65], we use all the splits (Spatial, Object, Goal, and Long) in Libero to train the model. In total, it has 272K training samples. Base VLM. We use the same architecture for the video generator (VideoDiT) and the action generator (ActionDiT) as the FastWAM. We follow FastWAMs implementation to initialize the model before the training. Base Predictor. We use 2-layer Qformer, with 16 learnable queries, 8 heads, hidden dimension 1024, to aggregate the information from the embedded text embedding and the vision token encoded by the VAE [53], and then we apply mean operation on the query tokens from the Qformer. We send the averaged query token to MLP to predict the ˆy. Flow Refiner. We use the ActionDiT in the FastWAM structure as the Flow Refiner, and we optimize the Flow Refiner with MSE loss between the target velocity and the predicted velocity vθ(y(t), t, z). Training, Inference and Evaluation. We use the same training and evaluation setting as FastWAM. We evaluate DRIFT on Libero-Spatial, Libero-Object, Libero-Goal, and Libero-Long. On Libero, we run 50 trials for each of the 10 subtasks in every suite. We report the success rate as the metric. All the experiments are trained on 8*H200 in 24 hours.

sections.title: C Additional Visualization
sections.content: Figure 5 presents additional visualizations on TVG, comparing the start and end timestamps predicted by different modules. Samples 14 show that DRIFT achieves more accurate temporal boundaries by iteratively refining the coarse prediction using the flow refiner. Samples 56 illustrate error patterns shared by all decoders. These errors are mainly caused by ambiguous or inaccurate annotations: in 22 Figure 5: Additional visualization on TVG. Action Decoder Libero-Long Flow DRIFT (w/ rand Base Predictor) DRIFT 94.2 94.0 96.4 Table 6: With random base predictor (0% accuracy), DRIFT falls back to flow matching and shows similar performance. Sample 5, the action takes bite of the sandwich lasts only about two seconds, but the ground-truth boundary annotates it as five-second interval; in Sample 6, the query sits on bed is ambiguous, as it can refer either to the action of sitting down or to the state of already being seated. These cases suggest that further improving TVG performance may require both more carefully curated training and validation annotations, as well as models that are better able to handle ambiguous natural-language queries.

sections.title: D Additional ablation
sections.content: We follow the setting used in section 4.4 for the following experiment. Corrupted Base Predictor. In Table 6, we construct deliberately weak predictor. Specifically, we train DRIFT without applying the auxiliary loss Lp to the base predictor (MLP), while also stopping gradients from the refinement module to the base predictor. The base predictor thus remains untrained throughout optimization and achieves 0% accuracy on Libero-Long. Under this setting, DRIFT effectively degenerates to flow matching model and achieves comparable performance.
